item.title: Bears Keep Attacking People in Japan. We Traveled There to Find Out Why.
item.dc:creator: Owen Clarke

item.title: Did His Obsession with Dark Shamans Kill Him? I Traced This Anthropologist's Footsteps to Find Out.
item.dc:creator: Frank Bures

item.title: Forget Your Permit? These 10 Epic Adventures Don’t Require Reservations.
item.dc:creator: Madison Chapman

item.title: At a Tree-Climbing Competition, the Most Important Rule Is Don't Look Down
item.dc:creator: Calin Van Paris

item.title: The 10 Public Lands We’re on the Verge of Losing
item.description: From the most iconic national park to the newest national monument, much of America’s 640 million acres of public land is under siege. Here are the most endangered places, what we stand to lose, and our best chances for saving them.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Pearson

item.title: Why Millions of People Are Now Traveling to Find the World’s Last Dark Skies
item.description: From stargazing to aurora hunting, travelers are chasing an increasingly rare resource: natural night.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Vermillion

item.title: “I’m Actually Really Trying Not to Die." Charlize Theron on Her Upcoming Film, ‘Apex.’
item.description: From ‘Mad Max’ to ‘Atomic Blonde,’ Oscar winner Charlize Theron has built a legacy on punishing physicality. In her new survival thriller ‘Apex,’ she ups the ante as a climber hunted by a human predator, ditching stunt doubles to scale rock barefoot. She opens up about raw feet, the thin line between brave and reckless, and why misery makes for the best movies.
item.dc:creator: Abigail Wise

item.title: Heli-Skiing in California Seemed Like a Pipe Dream. Then These Two Guys Showed Up.
item.description: Two ski industry mavericks believe they’ve discovered the secret of operating a heli-skiing business in the Golden State, where red tape, finicky snowfall, and plenty of danger have torpedoed past operations
item.dc:creator: Brent Rose

item.title: How I Battled the Sizing Gap in Ski Gear—Even After Losing 100 Pounds
item.description: I realized that gear isn’t just gear. It’s a gatekeeper. And gear that hugs and tugs in all the wrong places doesn’t whisper. It declares: this ain’t for you, girl.
item.dc:creator: Marisa McMillan

item.title: ‘Be Intentional with Risk’ and Other Hard-Earned Advice from Alex Honnold
item.description: Honnold talks ‘Skyscraper Live,’ his relationship with the core climbing community, and how he assesses risk and danger as a 40-year-old father of two
item.dc:creator: Frederick Dreier

item.title: At Minnesota’s Coldest Ice Fishing Derby, You Don’t Want to Fall in
item.description: Outdoor recreation in Minnesota isn’t for the faint of heart. We spent a day at an ice fishing competition in the Land of 10,000 Lakes, where Mother Nature calls the shots, the fabric of community is thicker than denim, and tenacity is everything.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Pearson

item.title: What I Learned After Spending 14 Hours at a Suburban Ice Rink
item.description: A full day inside a suburban rink revealed how one place can hold ambition, grief, faith, and escape—sometimes all at once.
item.dc:creator: Blair Braverman

item.title: 200 Degrees and Sweaty Strangers. Welcome to Sauna Fest.
item.description: An honest account of my questionable journey into the hot-and-cold chaos of the Seattle Sauna Festival.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Arvesen

item.title: EXCLUSIVE: Will Smith’s Hardest Journey Yet Takes Him to the Ends of the Earth
item.description: In his new docuseries, 'Pole to Pole,' the actor travels through jungles, deserts, and polar ice. Outside sits down with Smith to talk about how filming the show pushed him to reckon with self-discovery and facing fear.
item.dc:creator: Abigail Wise

item.title: At 41, Lindsey Vonn Is Redefining What an Athlete Can Be
item.description: Freed from years of pain by a knee replacement—she’s back racing at 41, chasing Olympic glory in Cortina. Here’s how one of the world’s most dominant skiers is reclaiming what her legacy means beyond the podium.
item.dc:creator: Sierra Shafer

item.title: Joel Edgerton Talks ‘Train Dreams,’ Nature, and Finding Peace in the Wild
item.description: The Australian actor grew up on the edge of a forest and never quite left it behind. Now, with ‘Train Dreams’ on Netflix, he’s exploring what it means to live—and lose—our connection to the wild.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Sintumuang

item.title: Raise Your Glasses. Now, Hold Them Up Until Your Arms Shake.
item.description: Five pounds of lager, one outstretched arm, and the slow creep of regret. This is Type 2 fun at its finest.
item.dc:creator: Martin Fritz Huber

item.title: More National Park Service Rangers Are Voting to Unionize. Will It Save Their Jobs?
item.description: We spoke to labor experts and multiple NPS rangers about the push to unionize, and whether it can protect Park Service jobs from the federal government’s cutbacks
item.dc:creator: Frederick Reimers

item.title: To Hold His Breath for 24 Minutes, a Freediver Learned to Turn Off His Brain
item.description: After his daughter's diagnosis, Budimir Šobat gave up drinking and devoted himself to her care. Years later, he found a new obsession: holding his breath longer than anyone in history.
item.dc:creator: Sean Williams

item.title: What I Saw in Yosemite Says Everything About the Future of Our National Parks
item.description: How a long weekend in the park revealed the uncertain future of America's public lands
item.dc:creator: Gloria Liu

item.title: The Rangers Are Not Alright
item.description: Here's what happens when the dedicated employees of Rocky Mountain National Park start to break down
item.dc:creator: Frederick Dreier

item.title: What It’s Really Like to Visit America’s Most Crowded National Park
item.description: Outside’s national parks columnist braved America’s busiest national park before a coalition of local governments and nonprofits had to chip in to keep the gates open
item.dc:creator: Graham Averill

item.title: 40 Years, Zero Kills. We Spent a Weekend with Colorado's Least-Successful Elk Hunter.
item.description: Meet Carl Cocchiarella, Colorado’s least-successful elk hunter. After four decades of near misses, he’s learned that killing an animal isn’t the best part of a hunt.
item.dc:creator: Devon O’Neil

item.title: Michelle Vanek Vanished on a Hike 19 Years Ago. This Is How We Finally Found Her Remains.
item.description: She was the only hiker ever to die while seeking the summit of central Colorado’s most famous 14er. A member of the successful search team investigates who she was, how her death—and her recovery 19 years later—impacted her family, and what we all need to consider before heading into the alpine.
item.dc:creator: Ted Katauskas

item.title: Outdoor Gear’s Upcycling Revolution Is Here. This Designer Is Leading the Charge.
item.description: Known for both her gorpcore experiments and collabs with big-name design brands, Nicole McLaughlin has bridged the gap between outdoor gear and high fashion—and could very well transform both for the better
item.dc:creator: Corey Buhay

item.title: Once a Boomer Staple, Cottage Cheese Is Now a Gen-Z Obsession
item.description: The white, clumpy curd was all the rage in the early 20th century, but it has recently made a comeback. Young people are putting it in everything from dips and pastries to ice cream. While once pushed as a meat alternative during the First World War, its current craze seems to be rooted in Zoomers’ quest to achieve #fitlife. So, what makes cottage cheese the protein-packed star of the moment?
item.dc:creator: Marisa McMillan

item.title: 32 Hours, 135 Miles, and 118 Degrees: What It Takes to Crew the Hardest Race on Earth
item.description: Every year, ultrarunners sign up to compete in a brutal race that stretches 135 miles across Death Valley in the high heat of summer. Because of its harsh conditions, the Badwater 135 is perhaps the most intensely crewed ultra in the world, and without these dedicated support teams, for most, the race would be impossible.
item.dc:creator: Devin Kelly

item.title: I Built a Successful Bike Racing Series. It Was No Joyride.
item.description: For 14 years Andrew Willis oversaw Austin’s Driveway Series, a weekly criterium race for amateur cyclists. The stress, financial pressure, and constant criticism upended his life.
item.dc:creator: Andrew WillisIan Dille

item.title: This is What Happens When You Drop a Gen-Z Kid From LA Into the Alaskan Wilderness
item.description: Ben Weissenbach's new book offers a thoughtful look at Alaska's enduring magic—and its rapidly changing climate.
item.dc:creator: Corey Buhay

item.title: Good Adventure Buddies Are Hard to Find. Here's What Top Explorers Look For.
item.description: Work. laundry. The weather. There are so many excuses to not get out there. But when you have a solid adventure buddy, the answer is always yes.
item.dc:creator: Tom Vanderbilt

item.title: Before Anyone Scales Mount Everest, These Workers Risk Their Lives
item.description: Mount Everest’s Icefall Doctors, the workers who build and maintain the route through the Khumbu Glacier, pursue a livelihood rooted in tradition and danger
item.dc:creator: Ben Ayers

item.title: The Real Albert Lin Is Even More Inspiring Than He is on TV
item.description: A relentless explorer and celebrated TV host, Lin has built his on-camera identity on movement, resilience, and inspiration. But on a volcano in Ecuador—surrounded by fellow amputees—he finally lets himself be seen.
item.dc:creator: Peter Frick-Wright

item.title: How the Ocean Taught Mike la Rocha to Love His Father—And Himself
item.description: In his new memoir, de la Rocha explores the meaning of vulnerability, manhood, and the healing power of the outdoors.
item.dc:creator: Mike de la Rocha

item.title: Thieves Are Making Off With Olympic National Forest's Rarest Trees
item.description: Timber thieves are a slippery bunch. Here's how cops uncovered an underground criminal ring in spanning the Pacific Northwest and cracked down to protect the state's ancient trees.
item.dc:creator: Nick Davidson

item.title: What Are the 100 Best Miles of the Appalachian Trail?
item.description: The Appalachian Trail Conservancy—the nonprofit that supports the United States’ most iconic footpath—turns 100 this year. To celebrate, AT thru-hikers Mary Beth "Mouse" Skylis and Grayson Haver Currin pick the 100 best miles of trail, spread out over 19 bite-size sections.
item.dc:creator: Grayson Haver CurrinMary Beth “Mouse” Skylis

item.title: He Was Nearly Killed Last Year—Now He’s Out to Win It All
item.description: After a near-fatal bike crash in April 2024, ultrarunner David Roche decided to go all in chasing big goals. Five months later, he shattered the Leadville Trail 100 record and now has his sights set on winning Western States.
item.dc:creator: Devin Kelly

item.title: The Future of Burials Is Wild, and It’s Already Here
item.description: The long (and long overdue) rise of the forest cemetery
item.dc:creator: Robert Moor

item.title: Why Do We Leave Notes on Top of Mountains? It’s Personal.
item.description: For centuries, people have left all sorts of notes in summit registers. I looked through 100 years of love letters and spontaneous exaltation, including my own family's, to find out why.
item.dc:creator: Meaghen Brown

item.title: The Cult of the Mountain-Town Weatherman
item.description: Alpine locales have their own microclimates, which makes forecasting a tricky business—and a local fixation. Who dares try their hand? A few brave amateur meteorologists. We talked to one of the most elusive to find out why.
item.dc:creator: Heather Hansman

item.title: A Middle-Aged Dad Visits Downhill Skiing’s Rowdiest Party. What Could Go Wrong?
item.description: Our writer endured boozy days, sleepless nights in a hostel, and edge-of-your-seat racing at Kitzbühel’s legendary Hahnenkamm
item.dc:creator: Devon O’Neil

item.title: How Chasing Vert Is Changing the Way We Ski
item.description: When even the hardest-core ski bums are logging their data, is there still room for fun?
item.dc:creator: Jesse Will

item.title: I Went on a Singles-Only Ski Trip—Chaos Ensued
item.description: Done with endless swiping on dating apps, more people are looking for love through in-person events. I traveled to one in the Alps with a group of rowdy singles in search of love.
item.dc:creator: Kassondra Cloos

item.title: My Grandfather Brought a Mysterious Sword Home from WWII Japan. Eighty Years Later, I Returned It.
item.description: When I was a kid, I was fascinated by a traditional katana my grandfather had brought home from Japan in 1945. Years later, I decided it was time to find the heirloom’s rightful owner.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Chroust

item.title: Why Your Favorite Small Gear Brands Might Not Survive This Year
item.description: Brands like Youer manufacture their gear exclusively in the United States for environmental, ethical, and practical reasons. Will that be enough in the face of rising costs and potential new tariffs?
item.dc:creator: Amelia Arvesen

item.title: They Were Tracking Threatened Turtles. They Found Human Bones Instead.
item.description: For decades, field technicians have scoured the Mojave Desert monitoring threatened tortoises. Their searches sometimes uncovered human remains. Our writer untangles a mystery dug up by the turtle counters.
item.dc:creator: Mark Sundeen

item.title: They Call Him Weird Foothill Guy. His Style of Skiing Will Blow Your Mind.
item.description: Alex Kaufman, a suburban dad in Denver, descends slopes with barely any snow, using discontinued plastic skis. This method, he says, is far more fun than a day at the resort, so we accompanied him on an outing.
item.dc:creator: Frederick Dreier

item.title: Shailene Woodley Wants You to Touch Grass
item.description: We spent a night under the stars with the actress and environmentalist, who opened up about her conservation work and how nature helped heal her broken heart
item.dc:creator: Melissa Johnson

item.title: What I Learned About Fly-Fishing and Friendship on a Montana River
item.description: Change is inevitable. When it happens in our relationships, it’s best to take a cue from the currents and go with the flow.
item.dc:creator: David Quammen

item.title: Is Traveling to Every Country in the World a Worthy Cause? This Group Thinks So.
item.description: I tagged along on a surreal trip to a conflict zone in Azerbaijan with a group of explorers known as the world’s Most Traveled People. No matter that the war there wasn’t over yet.
item.dc:creator: Tim Neville

item.title: The Perfect Honeymoon Does Exist: A 200-Mile Walk Across England
item.description: My wife decided we needed an active outdoor getaway, a romantic ramble across moors and fells and three national parks. I knew it’d be hard. I’ve never been happier.
item.dc:creator: Steven Potter

item.title: I Chased the Northern Lights with One of Canada’s Best Indigenous Trackers
item.description: Joe Buffalo Child has a deep connection to the auroras, which his people, the Dene, believe carry messages from their ancestors. We headed into the boreal forest seeking light.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Vermillion

item.title: Why a Ski Mountaineer Is Running for Senate in Utah
item.description: Throughout her athletic career, Caroline Gleich has been moonlighting as an activist. This year, she stepped into politics full-time.
item.dc:creator: Anna Callaghan

item.title: Vacuum Pumps, Bandsaws, and a Real-Life Time Machine: Welcome to the Outside Gear Lab at CU Denver
item.description: Last fall, Outside partnered with University of Colorado Denver to open a state-of-the-art gear-testing lab. Now, it’s finally open for business—and poised to upend the gear-testing world.
item.dc:creator: Corey Buhay

item.title: The Heist that Nearly Sank a Beloved Outdoor Brand
item.description: How a small outdoor footwear company lost 5,000 pairs of shoes and found itself entangled in an international crime saga
item.dc:creator: Scott Yorko

item.title: This Valley Holds Some of the Last Old Growth in Montana. Why Would the Forest Service Want It Clear-Cut?
item.description: In the remote, heavily logged Yaak Valley, an unlikely stand of old growth sits at the center of a debate about what a forest is for—and how best to protect it
item.dc:creator: Laura Yale

item.title: Kimmy Fasani's New Film is a Masterclass in Resilience
item.description: In a new documentary, now screening at this year's Outside Festival, the professional snowboarder opens up about motherhood and her career in the shadow of a cancer diagnosis
item.dc:creator: Kate Siber

item.title: Want to Build a Perfect 21st Century Town? Ban Cars.
item.description: The community of Culdesac, Arizona, was designed for pedestrians and cyclists. And residents love it.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Zaleski

item.title: I Went to Yellowstone National Park to Learn Why It Turns Tourists into Morons
item.description: Petting bison, cooking food in geysers. Ride along with our writer on a wild trip to our nation’s most iconic national park at the height of tourist season to see all the bad behavior.
item.dc:creator: Drew Magary

item.title: I Needed to Stop Drinking. So I Hiked 100 Miles in Maine.
item.description: As part of a long struggle with alcoholism, I decided to jump-start my recovery with a serious physical challenge: hiking 100 miles of the Appalachian Trail
item.dc:creator: W. Hodding Carter

item.title: I Went Camping in 120-Degree Heat. The Experience Left Me Amazed—and Terrified.
item.description: As a brutal heat wave enveloped the country this summer, our writer packed up a cooler full of Gatorade and headed to the Mojave Desert
item.dc:creator: Leath Tonino

item.title: Is Hard Exercise the Formula for Longevity? Peter Attia Thinks So.
item.description: The longevity influencer, doctor, and bestselling author wants to change the way we take care of ourselves. Does it work?
item.dc:creator: Nick Heil

item.title: I Wrote Off Traveling with an Influencer. Then I Tried It.
item.description: New booking sites are connecting travel influencers with their followers to take trips all over the world. But should you go? I headed to Yosemite with an influencer and her fangirls to find out.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Levin

item.title: Whales Have Been Attacking Boats in Orca Alley. This Family Just Sailed Through It.
item.description: We’ve always been thrilled to see orcas near our home in Alaska. But sailing through the waters along the Iberian Peninsula, where 600 boats have been hit—and five sunk—by whales, was unnerving at best.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Van Hemert

item.title: Did a Dangerous New Mountaineering Trend Cause Four Deaths on Shishapangma?
item.description: Two American women and two Sherpa guides perished while racing for a record. The tragedy illuminates how the recent rush to climb the world’s highest peaks is driving climbers onto dangerous mountains like never before.
item.dc:creator: Gloria Liu

item.title: Sepp Kuss Is the Great American Cyclist We’ve Been Waiting For
item.description: A relaxed, charming kid from Durango may make Americans fall in love with the Tour de France again
item.dc:creator: Frederick Dreier

item.title: The Man Who Claims That Reinhold Messner Came Up Short
item.description: When Eberhard Jurgalski determined that Reinhold Messner narrowly missed a key summit, he told the world. He’s still dealing with the fallout.
item.dc:creator: Tristan Kennedy

item.title: I Hiked the Entire Length of the Grand Canyon. It Almost Killed Me.
item.description: In ‘A Walk in the Park,’ Kevin Fedarko’s new book about his quest to hike the big ditch from end to end, inadequate fitness and bad gear choices nearly led to disaster right from the start
item.dc:creator: Kevin Fedarko

item.title: The Forgotten Hero of the 1953 First Ascent of Mount Everest
item.description: When Tenzing Norgay and Edmund Hillary made history by reaching the summit, a courier named Ten Tsewang Sherpa ran 200 miles to Kathmandu to deliver the news. He died a few weeks later. His story has never been told—until now.
item.dc:creator: Peter Frick-Wright

item.title: How Everest Was Turned into an Industry
item.description: ‘Everest, Inc.,’ a new book from veteran outdoor journalist Will Cockrell, documents the mountain’s transformation, first by Western guides and climbers, and now by Sherpas and Nepalis
item.dc:creator: Dave Hahn

item.title: How I Dealt with My Multiple Sclerosis Diagnosis: First, Hike 500 Miles
item.description: I could barely walk, a condition doctors told me would likely deteriorate further. That prompted me to plan an adventure of a lifetime.
item.dc:creator: Dan Slater

item.title: I Spent 82 Hours Alone in a Dark Cave. Things Got Weird.
item.description: Embarking on four days of total blackout, inside the sensory equivalent of a tomb, our writer went on a dark-cave retreat, the same one that quarterback Aaron Rodgers did
item.dc:creator: Tim Neville

item.title: Guilty or Innocent? How a Sled Dog Named Blowhole Chewed His Way to Social Media Fame.
item.description: Meet the famous Alaskan husky—and Iditarod finisher—who got miffed at a musher and chomped her truck’s brake lines. (Allegedly. Because a lot of people think this pup is innocent.)
item.dc:creator: Blair Braverman

item.title: America Needs Wildland Firefighters More than Ever, but Attrition Is at Record Levels
item.description: Highly skilled firefighters are the last line of defense against wildfires, but that line is fraying because the government decided long ago that they’re not worth very much
item.dc:creator: Abe Streep

item.title: Christina Lustenberger Skis the Impossible
item.description: Lusti has built a career—and a life—on toughness and a preternatural ability to ski through puckering technical terrain. Her greatest challenge may be learning to let herself be soft.
item.dc:creator: Cassidy Randall

item.title: Welcome to I-70, Colorado’s Notoriously Gridlocked Ski Highway
item.description: Cars spin, trucks slide, and what should be an hour’s drive can take all day. How did this scenic mountain corridor get so congested—and can it ever be fixed? I took a wild ride through the traffic jam to find out.
item.dc:creator: Gloria Liu

item.title: The Kids from Cool Lane Just Want to Ride Bikes
item.description: In May 2022, we took a spin with the Richmond Cycling Corps, a mountain-bike-racing team from the Virginia capital’s public-housing system. Coaches teach young riders how to shred trails and prepare for adult life. The kids, meanwhile, measure happiness one pedal stroke at a time.
item.dc:creator: Ian Dille

item.title: What It Takes to Build the Most Dangerous Jumps in the World
item.description: At Red Bull Rampage, the infamous freeride mountain-bike event held each year in a remote corner of Utah, riders and teams construct their own runs, walking a fine line between death-defying and deadly
item.dc:creator: Tom Vanderbilt

item.title: “Please I Will Give Anything for You to Come Back”
item.description: Why did a mother with no backcountry experience take her sister and 13-year-old son to live off the grid on a 10,000-foot mountain during a Colorado winter?
item.dc:creator: Ted Conover

item.title: Did This Website Revolutionize Surfing or Kill a Part of Its Renegade Soul?
item.description: The forecasting behemoth Surfline redefined the art of scoring waves. But some say it’s to blame for increasingly crowded breaks and a pervasive fear of missing out.
item.dc:creator: Kyle DeNuccio

item.title: Skiers Are Paying Resorts Big Bucks for First Tracks on Powder Days. But Should Powder Be for Sale?
item.description: Rising predawn to chase powder is sacred in ski culture, but an increasing number of resorts are offering early-access programs for people who can afford them
item.dc:creator: Roger Marolt
